author: garyrong
content: One exception is manual chain rewind. Geth provides a RPC endpoint `debug_setHead` which allows you to rewind the chain back to arbitrary block. If the target block is prior to the pivot block, the local block will be directly rewound to Genesis and chain will be "stateless". After the call, the snap sync will be re-triggered

author: garyrong
content:
Yes it's true. We need to think about if it makes sense to still support it. In the snap/1, it's very easy to utilize the existing state data locally and state healer will fix the state inconsistency very quickly in this scenario. In snap/2, it's definitely more complicated.

- If the target block is after the pivot block and geth has corresponding reverse diffs available, then state can be reverted with these reverse diffs, no snap sync required;
- If the target block is prior to the pivot block and with no reverse diffs available
    - Shall we terminate the rewind? Any benefits by keeping doing this?
    - If we keep rewinding, the local state should be reset and run the snap/2 again

author: garyrong
content:
> i would assume the EL would only fetch the finalized state
It's not correct. There is no guarantee the last finalized state is still accessible over the network. 

Geth by default keep the states of latest 128 blocks, namely ~25 minutes time window for state sync. If the chain is un-finalized for whatever reason, then it's impossible to sync up to the last finalized state

author: nero_eth
content: today, snap sync takes downloads the state at HEAD-64, without any guarantees that we have finalized.
